title: Plucking and gutting a turkey
subtitle: Processing a home-reared bird from scald to oven-ready.
excerpt: A turkey plucked and dressed at home takes longer than a chicken and rewards patience more. The bird needs a proper scald to loosen the feathers, a firm hand to remove the wing and tail quills, and clean, careful evisceration to avoid contaminating the carcass.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Guidance on humane on-farm slaughter, gov.uk. RSPCA welfare guidelines for slaughter. Fearnley-Whittingstall, H., River Cottage Meat Book (2004), for processing guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Immersing a freshly killed bird in hot water at 60 to 65°C to loosen the feathers from the follicles. Correct temperature and timing are critical: too cool and feathers don't loosen; too hot or too long and the skin tears.
Removing the internal organs from a slaughtered bird. The gut, gizzard, liver, and heart are removed whole; the lungs must be scraped from the ribcage separately.

content.text: Processing a turkey is the same sequence as processing a chicken, kill, scald, pluck, eviscerate, hang, but scaled up considerably. The bird is heavier, the wing joints are tougher, and the tail feathers need more force to pull. Allow two hours for your first bird; with practice it comes down to forty-five minutes.

On-farm slaughter for own consumption is legal for turkeys in the UK without a licence, provided you own the bird and the meat is not sold. The method must be humane. The preferred method for a smallholder is neck dislocation (stun and kill simultaneously for birds under 5 kg) or use of a killing cone and neck cut.
The scald

Bring a container large enough to submerge the bird to 60 to 63°C. A dustbin or large stock pot works. Immerse the bird breast-down for sixty to ninety seconds, agitating gently. Check a flight feather: it should pull free with moderate resistance. If it pulls without any resistance, the skin is over-scalded and will tear during plucking.
For a large bronze turkey over 8 kg, a two-stage
scald

is more reliable: sixty seconds, partial pluck on the breast and thighs, then a second sixty-second scald for the wings and back. The breast skin on a large bird cooks easily in a prolonged single scald.
Plucking

Pluck against the direction of growth with short sharp pulls close to the skin surface. Long pulls pull the skin. Work from the breast outward. The breast feathers come off easily after a good scald; the flight feathers and tail feathers need individual pulls and more force.

The wing quills will not come out with scalding alone. Grip each one at the base and pull firmly. You can use pliers for particularly stubborn quills. Any remaining pin feathers come out with tweezers; singe any down remnants with a blowtorch or gas hob.
Evisceration
The
evisceration

procedure for a turkey follows the same steps as for a chicken, remove the crop and windpipe from the neck end, then open the carcass at the vent and draw the contents out intact. The turkey's organs are proportionally the same; the gut is larger and requires more care not to nick it.

Remove the oil gland above the tail before anything else. The tail can then be removed with the pygostyle by cutting through the last vertebra, which removes the gland cleanly. This is important on a turkey whose tail has not been trimmed, the oil gland is larger and more prominent than on a chicken.
The lungs sit in pockets along the ribcage and don't come out with the main gut pull. Use your fingers or a lung scraper to remove them; any lung remnant left inside will speed spoilage.
Hanging and storage

Hang the dressed bird in a cool larder (1 to 4°C) for three to five days before freezing or roasting. Hanging allows rigor mortis to resolve and improves the texture. Wrap loosely in muslin; do not seal in plastic. A turkey hanging at the wrong temperature or for too long will smell noticeably, if in doubt, cook sooner rather than later.
The
plucking and gutting a chicken
tutorial covers the same procedure in more detail.
